From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Register of real estate boundaries

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8b/FR_schloss_kinsky.jpg/250px-FR_schloss_kinsky.jpg)](/wiki/File:FR_schloss_kinsky.jpg)

Cadastral map from 1827 in Austria ([Franziszeischer cadastre](/wiki/Franziszeischer_cadastre "Franziszeischer cadastre"))

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Combined_Land_Parcels_of_Eastern_England_from_English_%26_Welsh_Cadastral_Data.png/250px-Combined_Land_Parcels_of_Eastern_England_from_English_%26_Welsh_Cadastral_Data.png)](/wiki/File:Combined_Land_Parcels_of_Eastern_England_from_English_%26_Welsh_Cadastral_Data.png)

A modern cadastral map for Eastern England

A **cadastre** or **cadaster** ([/kəˈdæstər/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*kə-DAS-tər*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")) is a comprehensive recording of the [real estate](/wiki/Real_estate "Real estate") or [real property](/wiki/Real_property "Real property")'s [metes-and-bounds](/wiki/Metes_and_bounds "Metes and bounds") of a country.[[1]](#cite_note-CTdata-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Henssen1995-2)
Often it is represented graphically in a **cadastral map**.

In most countries, legal systems have developed around the original administrative systems and use the cadastre to define the dimensions and location of land parcels described in legal documentation.
A **land parcel** or **cadastral parcel** is defined as "a continuous area, or more appropriately volume, that is identified by a unique set of homogeneous property rights".[[3]](#cite_note-dale-3)

Cadastral surveys document the [boundaries](/wiki/Boundary_(real_estate) "Boundary (real estate)") of land ownership, by the production of documents, diagrams, sketches, plans (*[plats](/wiki/Plat "Plat")* in the US), charts, and maps. They were originally used to ensure reliable facts for land valuation and taxation. An example from early [England](/wiki/England "England") is the [Domesday Book](/wiki/Domesday_Book "Domesday Book") in 1086. [Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon") established a comprehensive cadastral system for France that is regarded as the [forerunner](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/forerunner "wikt:forerunner") of most modern versions.

Cadastral survey information is often a base element in [Geographic Information Systems](/wiki/Geographic_Information_Systems "Geographic Information Systems") (GIS) or [Land Information Systems](/wiki/Land_Information_Systems "Land Information Systems") (LIS) used to assess and manage land and built infrastructure. Such systems are also employed on a variety of other tasks, for example, to track long-term changes over time for geological or ecological studies, where land tenure is a significant part of the scenario.

The cadastre is a fundamental source of data in disputes and [lawsuits](/wiki/Lawsuit "Lawsuit") between landowners.
[Land registration](/wiki/Land_registration "Land registration") and cadastre are both types of land recording and complement each other.[[2]](#cite_note-Henssen1995-2)

By clearly assigning property rights and demarcating land, cadasters have been attributed with strengthening state fiscal capacity and economic growth.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/KM_Bozen_Bl_1.jpg/250px-KM_Bozen_Bl_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:KM_Bozen_Bl_1.jpg)

Cadastral map of [Bozen](/wiki/Bozen "Bozen"), Tirol, Brixner Kreis Nr 1, 1858

## Etymology and definition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cadastre&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology and definition")]

The word *[cadastre](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/cadastre "wikt:cadastre")* came into [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") through French from the [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language") *katástikhon* (κατάστιχον), a list or register, from *katà stíkhon* (κατὰ στίχον)—literally, "(organised) line by line".[[5]](#cite_note-5)

A cadastre commonly includes details of the [ownership](/wiki/Ownership "Ownership"), the [tenure](/wiki/Land_tenure "Land tenure"), the precise location, the dimensions (and area), the cultivations if rural, and the [value](/wiki/Value_(economics) "Value (economics)") of individual parcels of land. Cadastres are used by many nations around the world, some in conjunction with other records, such as a title register.[[1]](#cite_note-CTdata-1)

The [International Federation of Surveyors](/wiki/International_Federation_of_Surveyors "International Federation of Surveyors") defines cadastre as follows:[[6]](#cite_note-6)

> A Cadastre is normally a parcel-based, and up-to-date land information system containing a record of interests in land (e.g. rights, restrictions and responsibilities). It usually includes a geometric description of land parcels linked to other records describing the nature of the interests, the ownership or control of those interests, and often the value of the parcel and its improvements.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cadastre&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

See also: [Surveying § History](/wiki/Surveying#History "Surveying"), [Cartography § History](/wiki/Cartography#History "Cartography"), and [Topographic mapping § History](/wiki/Topographic_mapping#History "Topographic mapping")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/A_cadastre_text_written_in_Akkadian._Terracotta_tablet._18th_century_BCE._From_Sippar%2C_Iraq._Ancient_Orient_Museum%2C_Istanbul.jpg/250px-A_cadastre_text_written_in_Akkadian._Terracotta_tablet._18th_century_BCE._From_Sippar%2C_Iraq._Ancient_Orient_Museum%2C_Istanbul.jpg)](/wiki/File:A_cadastre_text_written_in_Akkadian._Terracotta_tablet._18th_century_BCE._From_Sippar,_Iraq._Ancient_Orient_Museum,_Istanbul.jpg)

A cadastre text written in Akkadian on a terracotta tablet; from the 18th century BC in Sippar, Iraq, and held by the Ancient Orient Museum, Istanbul

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/Katastermarke_s%C3%BCdtirol_2018.jpg/250px-Katastermarke_s%C3%BCdtirol_2018.jpg)](/wiki/File:Katastermarke_s%C3%BCdtirol_2018.jpg)

Cadastre survey marker from the [South Tyrol](/wiki/South_Tyrol "South Tyrol") mountains, 2018

Some of the earliest cadastres were ordered by [Roman Emperors](/wiki/Roman_Emperors "Roman Emperors") to recover state owned lands that had been appropriated by private individuals, and thereby recover income from such holdings. One such cadastre was done in AD 77 in Campania, a surviving stone marker of the survey reads "The Emperor [Vespasian](/wiki/Vespasian "Vespasian"), in the eighth year of his tribunician power, so as to restore the state lands which the Emperor [Augustus](/wiki/Augustus "Augustus") had given to the soldiers of Legion II Gallica, but which for some years had been occupied by private individuals, ordered a survey map to be set up with a record on each 'century' of the annual rental".[[7]](#cite_note-Kain-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) In this way Vespasian was able to reimpose taxation formerly uncollected on these lands.[[7]](#cite_note-Kain-7)

With the fall of Rome, the use of cadastral maps effectively discontinued. Medieval practice used written descriptions of the extent of land rather than using more precise surveys. Only in the sixteenth and early seventeenth centuries did the use of cadastral maps resume, beginning in the Netherlands. With the emergence of [capitalism](/wiki/Capitalism "Capitalism") in [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance") Europe, the need for cadastral maps reemerged as a tool to determine and express control of land as a means of production. This took place first privately in land disputes and later spread to governmental practice as a means of more precise tax assessment.[[7]](#cite_note-Kain-7)

## Surveys

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cadastre&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Surveys")]

This section is an excerpt from [Cadastral surveying](/wiki/Cadastral_surveying "Cadastral surveying").[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Cadastral_surveying&action=edit)]

[Cadastral surveying](/wiki/Cadastral_surveying "Cadastral surveying") is the sub-field of cadastre and [surveying](/wiki/Surveying "Surveying") that specialises in the establishment and re-establishment of [real property](/wiki/Real_estate "Real estate") boundaries. It involves the physical delineation of property boundaries and determination of dimensions, areas and certain rights associated with properties. This is regardless of whether they are on land, water or defined by natural or artificial features.[[9]](#cite_note-9) It is an important component of the legal creation of properties. A cadastral surveyor must apply both the spatial-measurement principles of general surveying and legal principles such as respect of neighboring titles.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Bureau_of_Land_Management_Cadastral_Survey_Marker_Arizona.jpg/250px-Bureau_of_Land_Management_Cadastral_Survey_Marker_Arizona.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bureau_of_Land_Management_Cadastral_Survey_Marker_Arizona.jpg)

BLM cadastral survey marker from 1992 in [San Xavier, Arizona](/wiki/San_Xavier,_Arizona "San Xavier, Arizona")

## Map

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cadastre&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Map")]

See also: [Plat](/wiki/Plat "Plat")

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **does not [cite](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources") any [sources](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this section](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Cadastre "Special:EditPage/Cadastre") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and [removed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Burden_of_evidence "Wikipedia:Verifiability"). *(August 2024)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

A *cadastral map* is a [map](/wiki/Map "Map") that shows the [boundaries](/wiki/Border "Border") and ownership of land parcels. Some cadastral maps show additional details, such as survey district names, unique identifying numbers for parcels, certificate of title numbers, positions of existing structures, section or lot numbers and their respective areas, adjoining and adjacent street names, selected boundary dimensions and references to prior maps.

[James C. Scott](/wiki/James_C._Scott "James C. Scott"), in *Seeing Like a State*, argues that all maps, but particularly cadastral maps, are designed to make local situations legible to an outsider, and in doing so, enable states to collect data on their subjects. He sees the origins of this in [Early Modern Europe](/wiki/Early_Modern_Europe "Early Modern Europe"), where [taxation](/wiki/Tax "Tax") became more complex. Cadastral maps, he argues, are always a great simplification, but they in themselves help change reality.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

## Documentation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cadastre&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Documentation")]

Cadastral documentation comprises the documentary materials submitted to cadastre or land administration offices for renewal of cadastral recordings. Cadastral documentation is kept in paper and/or electronic form.[[11]](#cite_note-11) Jurisdiction statutes and further provisions specify the content and form of the documentation,[[12]](#cite_note-12) as well as the person(s) authorized to prepare and sign the documentation, including concerned parties (owner, etc.), licensed [surveyors](/wiki/Surveying#Profession "Surveying") and legal advisors. The office concerned reviews the submitted information; if the documentation does not comply with stated provisions, the office may set a deadline for the applicant to submit complete documentation.[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)

The concept of cadastral documentation emerged late in the English language, as the institution of cadastre developed outside English-speaking countries. In a Danish textbook, one out of fifteen chapters regards the form and content of documents concerning subdivision and other land matters.[[15]](#cite_note-15) Early textbooks of international scope focused on recordings in terms of [land registration](/wiki/Land_registration "Land registration") and technical aspects of [cadastral survey](/wiki/Cadastral_survey "Cadastral survey"), yet note that 'cadastral surveying has been carried out within a tight framework of legislation'.[[16]](#cite_note-Dale1-16)[[3]](#cite_note-dale-3) With the view of assessing transaction costs, a European project: Modelling real property transactions (2001–2005) charted procedures for the transfer of ownership and other rights in land and buildings.[[17]](#cite_note-17) Cadastral documentation is described, e.g. for Finland as follows '8. Surveyor draws up cadastral map and cadastral documents … 10. Surveyor sends cadastral documents to cadastral authority.' [[18]](#cite_note-18) In Australia, similar activities are referred to as 'lodgement of plans of subdivision at land titles offices' [[19]](#cite_note-19)

## Management

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cadastre&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Management")]

Cadastre management has been used by the software industry since at least 2005.[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21) It mainly refers to the use of technology for management of cadastre and land information in [geographic information systems](/wiki/Geographic_information_system "Geographic information system"), [spatial data infrastructures](/wiki/Spatial_data_infrastructure "Spatial data infrastructure") and [software architecture](/wiki/Software_architecture "Software architecture"), rather than to general management issues of cadastral and other land information agencies.[[16]](#cite_note-Dale1-16)

### United Kingdom

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cadastre&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: United Kingdom")]

In Scotland there is a Cadastral Map: Land Registration etc (Scotland) Act 2012. In 1836, [Colonel Robert Dawson](/wiki/Robert_K._Dawson_(surveyor) "Robert K. Dawson (surveyor)") of the [Royal Engineers](/wiki/Royal_Engineers "Royal Engineers") proposed that a cadastre be implemented in light of his experiences as on secondment to the [Tithe Commission](/w/index.php?title=Tithe_Commission&action=edit&redlink=1 "Tithe Commission (page does not exist)").

In England, Wales and Northern Ireland there is a system of [land registration](/wiki/Land_registration "Land registration") with similar functions, but the word "cadastre" is not used.

### United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cadastre&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: United States")]

In the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), cadastral survey within the [Bureau of Land Management](/wiki/Bureau_of_Land_Management "Bureau of Land Management") (BLM) maintains records of all [public lands](/wiki/Public_land "Public land"). Such surveys often require detailed investigation of the history of land use, legal accounts, and other documents.

The [Public Lands Survey System](/wiki/Public_Lands_Survey_System "Public Lands Survey System") is a cadastral survey of the United States originating in legislation from 1785, after [international recognition](/wiki/Diplomatic_recognition "Diplomatic recognition") of the United States. The [Dominion Land Survey](/wiki/Dominion_Land_Survey "Dominion Land Survey") is a similar cadastral survey conducted in Western Canada, begun in 1871 after the creation of the Dominion of Canada in 1867. Both cadastral surveys are made relative to [principal meridian](/wiki/Principal_meridian "Principal meridian") and [baselines](/wiki/Baseline_(surveying) "Baseline (surveying)"). These cadastral surveys divided the surveyed areas into [townships](/wiki/Survey_township "Survey township"). Some much earlier surveys in Ohio created 25 square mile townships when the design of the system was being explored. Later, the design became square land areas of approximately 36 square miles (six miles by six miles). These townships are divided into [sections](/wiki/Section_(United_States_land_surveying) "Section (United States land surveying)"), each approximately one-mile square. Unlike in Europe, this cadastral survey largely preceded settlement and as a result greatly influenced settlement patterns. Properties are generally rectangular, boundary lines often run on cardinal bearings, and parcel dimensions are often in fractions or multiples of [chains](/wiki/Chain_(length) "Chain (length)"). [Land descriptions](/wiki/Land_description "Land description") in Western North America are principally based on these land surveys.

## Extensions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cadastre&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Extensions")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [[icon]](/wiki/File:Wiki_letter_w_cropped.svg) | This section **needs expansion**. You can help by [adding missing information](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Cadastre&action=edit&section=). *(August 2024)* |

Extensions of the conventional cadastre concept include the **3D cadastre**, considering the vertical domain;[[22]](#cite_note-22) and the **multipurpose cadastre**, considering non-parcel data.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

According to the [UN Economic Commission for Europe](/wiki/United_Nations_Economic_Commission_for_Europe "United Nations Economic Commission for Europe"), a "**Marine Cadastre** describes the location and spatial extent of rights, restrictions and responsibilities in the marine environment".[[24]](#cite_note-:1-24)
Marine cadastres apply the same governance principles to the water.[[25]](#cite_note-:0-25) They help further conservation and sustainability efforts.[[25]](#cite_note-:0-25) This is especially a concern in Europe's large aquatic market.[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[24]](#cite_note-:1-24) In Australia, they are used by many parties to plan around legal, technical, and institutional considerations.[[27]](#cite_note-27)
A related concept is that of *marine [spatial data infrastructures](/wiki/Spatial_data_infrastructure "Spatial data infrastructure")*.[[28]](#cite_note-28)
